summary: DeVault reviews the Pinebook Pro after years of using an 11-year-old ThinkPad X200 as his sole laptop. Despite significant frustrations with the ARM architecture, missing kernel support, and various hardware shortcomings like a poor touchpad and weak speakers, he finds the Pinebook Pro to be the first laptop in a decade that meaningfully improves on the state of the art for his needs. He praises its weight, screen quality, battery life, user-serviceability, and adequate performance for his workflows. The device will largely replace his ThinkPad, though ARM limitations keep the old machine relevant for niche uses.
key_points: ARM remains a frustrating architecture due to poor upstream Linux support, though the Pine community is steadily improving the situation
The Pinebook Pro succeeds where modern laptops fail by being lightweight, repairable, and functional without excess
Performance is acceptable for parallelizable builds in languages like C and Hare, but not competitive with x86_64
User-serviceability is a major selling point — easy disassembly with standard tools and replaceable parts
Despite numerous hardware compromises, the overall package is the first meaningful laptop improvement DeVault has seen in ten years
money_quotes: The state of laptops in $CURRENTYEAR is abysmal.
I am pleased to share that the Pinebook Pro is a pleasure to use, and is likely to finally replace the old ThinkPad for most of my needs.
ARM is still a nightmare to work with. I really hate this architecture.
Browsing the web is a bit of a chore, but it always was.
The device is user-servicable, which I appreciate very much.
It is the only laptop I have ever used which makes a substantial improvement on the circa-2010 state of the art.
It checks most of my boxes, which is something no other laptop has even come remotely close to in the past ten years.
key_insight: The Pinebook Pro is the first laptop in a decade to meaningfully advance beyond the circa-2010 ThinkPad standard, despite ARM's ongoing pain points, because it prioritizes repairability, simplicity, and openness over raw specs.
